summary: DHH argues that web developers don't need JavaScript-heavy single-page application frameworks to build modern, responsive web apps. Instead, generating HTML on the server and sending it directly to the browser—what he calls 'HTML Over The Wire'—offers a radically simpler approach. He contends that the JavaScript-everything trend of the past decade has created unnecessary complexity that particularly burdens small teams. The post introduces Hotwire as a concrete implementation of this philosophy, requiring only small JavaScript libraries rather than full client-side MVC frameworks.
key_points: Server-generated HTML delivered directly to the browser can replace JSON APIs and client-side MVC frameworks for most web applications
The JavaScript-everything approach creates incidental complexity that disproportionately hurts small teams, even if large companies can absorb it
HTML Over The Wire restores language diversity on the server side, freeing developers from writing everything in JavaScript
A small amount of JavaScript is still needed for polish and interactivity, but it doesn't need to be the center of the architecture
This approach lowers the barrier to entry for newcomers overwhelmed by modern frontend toolchain complexity
money_quotes: You can write fast, modern, responsive web applications by generating your HTML on the server, and delivering that (with a little help) directly to the browser.
But just because you can, doesn't mean you should.
The price for pursuing JavaScript for everything has been a monstrosity of modern complexity.
But incidental complexity can absolutely break your back if you're a small team where everyone has to do a lot.
The tools and techniques forged in the belly of a huge company is often the exact opposite of what you need to make progress at your scale.
When we embrace HTML as the format to send across the wire, we liberate ourselves from having to write all the code that creates that HTML in JavaScript.
A throwback to a time where you could view source and make sense of it.
key_insight: Most web applications don't need JavaScript-heavy architectures; sending server-rendered HTML directly to the browser eliminates incidental complexity and returns the web to its simpler, more diverse roots.
